summary: Dan Shapiro shares practical advice for amateur woodworkers on sourcing cheap lumber directly from local sawyers rather than retail hardwood stores. He explains that prices can be less than half of retail, with the added benefits of matched boards from single trees and more interesting character wood. The post walks through the key consideration of moisture content, covering kiln-dried, air-dried, and wet wood scenarios. He warns that wet wood requires proper storage and carries risk of cracking and warping, and advises buyers to verify moisture claims with a meter rather than trusting the seller. The post concludes with a concrete example: 100 board feet of air-dried maple for $0.70/bf, cheaper than construction-grade 2x4s.
key_points: Local sawyers offer hardwood at less than half retail prices, with better board matching from single-tree sourcing
Moisture content is the critical variable when buying from sawyers: kiln-dried (6-8%) is ideal, air-dried (10-12%) is acceptable, wet wood is risky even at steep discounts
Air-drying requires one year per inch of thickness with proper stacking and stickering under cover
Buyers should verify moisture claims independently rather than trusting the sawyer's word
A sawyer who hasn't painted log ends to prevent splitting is a red flag for incompetence
money_quotes: Not an ED ad, this is a serious subject for amateur sawdust-makers.
Usually all your wood comes from one tree, so the boards match much better than what you get from the store.
It doesn't matter how long the log has been sitting around, since the wood only really dries when properly stacked and stickered.
If the sawyer hasn't painted the ends of his logs, run-don't-walk since he has no clue what he's doing.
The average sawyer is an enterprising businessman, and as such, may well be lying to you, especially about the moisture content.
I just bought 100 bf of airdried 8/4 maple for $0.70/bf, less than the cost of an equivalent volume of 2x4s.
key_insight: Buying lumber directly from local sawyers can dramatically reduce costs for woodworkers, but requires understanding moisture content and drying methods to avoid expensive mistakes.
